source_claim: The queen's dream presents falling sceptre and parasol trembling gate bell darkness and celestial disorder as warnings of royal collapse
Attendants guards elders and bearers of ornaments textiles cosmetics garlands fans and incense accompany the queen into court
Kaṇṇaki publicly names the king as one who failed royal justice and demands admission while holding the paired anklet's survivor
Kaṇṇaki establishes her name Pukār origin merchant lineage marriage and the purpose for which Kōvalaṉ entered Madurai
Her legal argument rests on a testable difference: her anklet contains gems while the queen's anklet contains pearls
Kaṇṇaki breaks the anklet before the court and a gem striking the king provides immediate physical proof
The king identifies himself rather than Kōvalaṉ as the thief because sovereign negligence caused the unlawful killing
The queen bows at Kaṇṇaki's feet and falls after declaring that no consolation can be shown to a woman who has lost her husband
Kaṇṇaki invokes remembered women whose fidelity was demonstrated through public ordeal rescue sacrifice or transformation
She declares that if she is truly a pattini she will not spare the ruler or Madurai
Kaṇṇaki addresses women men gods and ascetics circles Madurai three times and casts away her left breast as the embodied act of accusation
The fire deity appears in Brahmin form and states that an earlier command authorised burning the city when it wronged the great pattini

anchor_phrase: பார்ப்பார் அறவோர் பசு பத்தினிப் பெண்டிர் மூத்தோர் குழவி
source_claim: Kaṇṇaki orders fire to spare ritual specialists righteous people cattle faithful wives elders and children
Fire is directed toward those aligned with wrongdoing rather than described as indiscriminate natural disaster
Commanding and protective deities open or close their thresholds and withdraw as palace institutions stand paralysed before the fire
